skill.name: remotion-render
skill.description: Render videos from React/Remotion component code via inference.sh. Pass TSX code, get MP4. Supports all Remotion APIs: useCurrentFrame, useVideoConfig, spring, interpolate, AbsoluteFill, Sequence. Configurable resolution, FPS, duration, codec. Use for: programmatic video generation, animated graphics, motion design, data-driven videos, React animations to video. Triggers: remotion, render video from code, tsx to video, react video, programmatic video, remotion render, code to video, animated video, motion graphics code, react animation video
skill.summary: Render Remotion React component code into MP4 videos using the inference.sh CLI.
skill.author: qu-skills

security_audit.summary: This skill is a documentation wrapper for the inference.sh belt CLI that renders Remotion React components into MP4 videos. The static analyzer flagged 32 instances of 'shell backtick execution' which are false positives - they are JavaScript template literals inside TSX code examples (e.g., `scale(${scale})`). The 6 hardcoded URLs are legitimate documentation links. The 3 'weak crypto' findings are false positives triggered by the word 'codec' in the input schema. The frontmatter restricts tool access to `Bash(belt *)` which is a proper sandboxing pattern. No prompt injection, data exfiltration, or malicious intent detected.

low_findings.title: External CLI tool dependency
low_findings.description: The skill requires the inference.sh `belt` CLI to be installed and authenticated. Users must run `belt login` and have network access to inference.sh endpoints. The skill's `allowed-tools: Bash(belt *)` restriction is good sandboxing practice.

content.value_statement: Programmatic video generation requires complex setup. This skill sends React/Remotion TSX code to inference.sh and returns a finished MP4 file with configurable resolution, FPS, and duration.
actual_capabilities: Render Remotion React component code into MP4 video files via inference.sh CLI
Support all core Remotion APIs: useCurrentFrame, useVideoConfig, spring, interpolate, AbsoluteFill, Sequence
Configure video output resolution, FPS, duration in seconds, and codec
Pass props to React components for data-driven video generation
Stream render progress updates in real time via Python SDK
Access the inference.sh platform through both Bash CLI and Python SDK
limitations: Requires inference.sh CLI (`belt`) to be installed and authenticated
Dependent on network access to inference.sh endpoints for rendering
Limited to the import paths documented (remotion and react modules only)
Free tier or paid inference.sh account needed depending on usage volume

use_cases.title: Generate animated explainer videos
use_cases.description: Create data-driven animated videos from React component code without setting up a full Remotion build pipeline.

use_cases.title: Build motion design prototypes
use_cases.description: Quickly prototype motion graphics and animated UI sequences by writing TSX and getting MP4 output in minutes.

use_cases.title: Automate video generation with Python
use_cases.description: Use the Python SDK to programmatically render batches of Remotion videos with streaming progress tracking.

prompt_templates.title: Basic fade-in text animation
prompt_templates.prompt: Render a 3-second video with the text 'Hello World' fading in on a black background. Use 1920x1080 resolution at 30 FPS.

prompt_templates.title: Animated progress counter
prompt_templates.description: Create a 5-second animated loading counter that shows percentage progress from 0 to 100.
prompt_templates.prompt: Render a 5-second square video (1080x1080) showing an animated percentage counter from 0% to 100% with a 'Loading...' subtitle below it.

prompt_templates.title: Spring-animated card
prompt_templates.description: Generate a video with a card that bounces in using Remotion's spring animation.
prompt_templates.prompt: Create a 2-second 1080x1080 video with an indigo background. A white rounded card should spring in from scale 0 to scale 1, centered on screen.

prompt_templates.title: Props-driven title sequence
prompt_templates.description: Build a video that accepts title and subtitle props for dynamic content generation.
prompt_templates.prompt: Render a 3-second 1920x1080 video that accepts 'title' and 'subtitle' props. Display the title in large white text and the subtitle in smaller gray text below, both centered on a black background.

output_examples.input: Render a 2-second welcome text animation on dark background
output: MP4 video file returned with video URL
Text fades from invisible to fully visible over the first second

output_examples.input: Create a spring-animated white card on indigo background
output: MP4 video file with scale animation from 0 to 1
Smooth bounce effect with damping: 10, stiffness: 100

best_practices: Always escape double quotes properly when embedding TSX code inside the JSON input string for belt commands
Use single quotes around the JSON argument in bash to avoid shell interpretation of double quotes inside the code
Test with short durations (2-3 seconds) and low resolution first to iterate quickly before producing final renders
anti_patterns: Do not try to import modules outside of remotion and react - the renderer only supports these import paths
Do not use backticks or shell metacharacters unescaped inside the code string, as they will break the JSON payload
Do not skip the `belt login` step - rendering requires an authenticated inference.sh account

faq.question: What is required to use this skill?
faq.answer: You need the inference.sh CLI tool called `belt` installed on your system and an authenticated account. Run `belt login` to authenticate before rendering.

faq.question: Which Remotion APIs are supported?
faq.answer: The renderer supports core Remotion APIs including useCurrentFrame, useVideoConfig, spring, interpolate, AbsoluteFill, Sequence, Audio, Video, and Img. You can also import standard React hooks.

faq.question: Can I use this with Python?
faq.answer: Yes, inference.sh provides a Python SDK. You can call `client.run()` with the `infsh/remotion-render` app and stream progress updates in real time.

faq.question: What output formats are available?
faq.answer: The renderer produces MP4 video files. You can configure the codec, resolution, FPS, and duration in the input parameters.

faq.question: Is there a way to track render progress?
faq.answer: Yes, when using the Python SDK you can pass `stream=True` to receive real-time progress percentage updates as the video renders.

faq.question: How do I pass dynamic data to my component?
faq.answer: Use the `props` parameter in the input JSON. Your component receives them as standard React props in its function signature.
